As it was originally written, this function did two things:

1. Find feasible matchings (hits) between ref and est under a specific definition of hit
2. Convert hits to a graph and find an optimal matching.

Maybe a better factorization of this functionality might be to just implement 2, and have a separate function for 1.  Then we can provide a few different hit-matrix constructors that all feed into the same matching alg.

I don't have strong opinions on this though, so maybe it's overkill to factor it that far.

The problem is that the explanation above works if you have one core. If you have two cores, a load of 2 is perfectly fine, it means each of your core had just enough work to keep them busy. You get the idea. Load is the average number of active process at once over the specified amount of time. So it's really a number of processes. If we really want to see it as percentage, we have to do `load * 100 / n_cpus`. And I really don't think it'll match people expectations. If for everything else, the threshold is compared against the value and for this value only it's compared against the load percent per cpu, that's going to surprise people.

Thinking aloud:
Is a class that inherits from a namedtuple really the best choice here?
My initial aversion is that it feels clunky. Also, now that we have 7 args, it strikes me that variable expansion is prone to breaking if we mess up the argument order somewhere and don't notice.
My preference would be to abandon the tuple aspect. And instead I think we should just have a class, and always access the members by name. Something like:

```
class FullArgSpec(object):
  """The full argspec of a callable."""

  def __init__(self, args=None, varargs=None, varkw=None, defaults=None,
               kwonlyargs=None, kwonlydefaults=None, annotations=None):
    self.args = args or []
    self.varargs = varargs
    self.varkw = varkw
    self.defaults = defaults or ()
    self.kwonlyargs = kwonlyargs or []
    self.kwonlydefaults = kwonlydefaults or {}
    self.annotations = annotations or {}
```

I looked into why the `InterpolatedChromaticObject` tests were failing, and ended up changing a few things.

The first was just the prescription for thinning and truncating the bandpasses.  It turned out that even the thinned bandpasses were keeping a pretty wide wavelength range, which led to the `InterpolatedChromaticObject` exerting a lot of effort to generate sample points at wavelengths that didn't really matter that much.  Explicitly truncating the bandpasses has helped with this while maintaining a reasonable looking throughput curve.  Here's the current r-band filter in blue, for instance, with the points used by `InterpolatedChromaticObject` in `test_ChromaticOpticalPSF` overplotted in red:

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14727552/e2a151f0-07e0-11e6-89f7-5833ff2ae56d.png)

I also looked into increasing the number of `InterpolatedChromaticObject` wavelength samples, and the oversampling.  It turns out that the oversampling tends to be more important than the number of samples, especially for the interpolated `ChromaticOpticalPSF`, which actually goes through two rounds of `InterpolatedImage`s (one for the `OpticalPSF`, and another for the `InterpolatedChromaticObject`).  These two changes have left me mostly happy with the chromatic unit tests, and in fact I was able to restore the previously degraded chromatic transformations back to where they started.

The part I'm not as happy about is the last test in `test_ChromaticOpticalPSF`, which compares an image sum with its expected flux from synthetic photometry.  This only works to about 1%, and I haven't figured out why it's not better (doesn't seem to matter what the number of interpolation samples or the oversampling factor are).  An existing comment had indicated that the goal here was 2%, though that's not exactly what was actually being tested for, so the 1% accuracy achieved above actually failed.  So I just rewrote the test so that it's actually testing for 2% and now everything passes.   :-/

That `sys.maxsize > 2 ** 32` implementation is copied from [official document](https://docs.python.org/3/library/platform.html#cross-platform). We used it without too much thought. And yes, at a first look, it would be a bug on truely 32-bit platform.

But, as it turns out, Python supports long integer even on 32-bit platform. So, we do not really need to choose that exact biggest 32-bit number, we simply need any number larger than 2**31-1

```
>>> import platform
>>> platform.architecture()[0]
'32bit'
>>> import sys
>>> sys.maxint
2147483647
>>> 2**31-1
2147483647L
>>> 2**32
4294967296L
>>> sys.maxint > 2** 32
False
```

PS: I did not notice all these until you raised this question. Thank you. Learn something new each day!

It seems that doing a set-true/set-false of `PROP_THREAD_ALLOW_LOCAL_NET_DATA_CHANGE` after all the changes isn't sufficient, due to the following code in both `InsertPropertyHandler_THREAD_LOCAL_ROUTES()` and `InsertPropertyHandler_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS()`, added in #273 (ping @abtink):

```
    VerifyOrExit(
        mAllowLocalNetworkDataChange == true,
        errorCode = SendLastStatus(header, SPINEL_STATUS_INVALID_STATE)
    );
```

This check is present to force the host implementation to use it in a way that is consistent with how we intended it to be used (Set this property to `true`, make your changes, then set it to `false`). I see three options to proceed forward:

1. Remove these checks from those functions in `ncp_base.cpp` and continue what you are doing.
2. Make the NCP automatically set `PROP_THREAD_ALLOW_LOCAL_NET_DATA_CHANGE` to `true` whenever a net data related property is modified.
3. Make sure your calls to add prefixes and local routes set `PROP_THREAD_ALLOW_LOCAL_NET_DATA_CHANGE` to true before setting the prefix. It is totally OK to set it to true multiple times.

I'm inclined to think option 3 would be the best since these checks force the host to interact with the NCP in the prescribed way—which should hopefully lead to better host implementations.

I'm not sure this description is a great fit for our DV certificates because "trust your identity" might have a lot of conceptual baggage that doesn't apply here. For example, if someone read this and then we asked "hey, if there's a certificate for greatregionalbank.com, does that mean we can trust that it's Great Regional Bank's web site?" or "hey, if there's a certificate for awesomesoftwaredownloads.net, does that mean we can trust that awesomesoftwaredownloads.net is a legitimate web site?".

I've struggled with this issue a lot because I think that certificates were *always* presented this way when they were first introduced to the web, yet in fact in our DV service, we almost don't want users to get *any* information from the certificate, we just want it to help the browser detect MITM attacks and refuse to connect. Beyond that, we add just about no security value, and may aspire to a future where the browser doesn't even show any distinctive UI for HTTPS connections, but shows a security warning for HTTP. In this case the certificate really doesn't aspire to communicate anything to the user in the course of a typical interaction, but it does still protect them by defending against surveillance and manipulation by the network intermediaries.

def add_filter(self, filter_values):
    # Perform a classic AND on values wihtout OR operator
    and_values = (v for v in filter_values if OR_OPERATOR not in v)
    and_filter = super(TermsFacet, self).add_filter(and_values)
    # Build a OR query for each value with OR
    or_values = (v for v in filter_values if OR_OPERATOR in v)
    or_filters = (
        any(Q('term',  , **{field_name: v}) for v in values.split(OR_OPERATOR))
        for values in or_values
    )  # Could be wrapped in all() but we do it once and for with and_filter
    return all(and_filter, *or_filters)
```

First, recall that we are likely to want to do a refresh operation before performing updates. See https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform/issues/57. As soon as we do that, we are going to be doing more "read"-like operations. It's worth considering how we will model those in the engine, given these changes.

Second, it occurs to me that we could actually just say that, instead of `OpRead` and `OpReadReplacement`, the existing operations `OpCreate`, `OpUpdate`, and `OpCreateReplacement` handle these functions. They would simply mean something different when `External` is `true`, just as `OpDelete` is different when `External` is `true`. The nice thing is that this may help inch us towards https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform/issues/57.

This is appealing to me because on the UI, it's not clear to me we want to expose these operations so prominently. So we'd just keep our existing visualizations and merely present them differently for external resources in a consistent way. I'm also sure *some* people will want to see them, but because they are external to the stack, it's probably far less interesting.  And, indeed, it may be alarming, since most people assume resources fetched in this manner are *not* under the management of Pulumi. If they see one getting deleted, they are likely to freak out. So I suspect we'd want to filter them out by default or summarize them in a specific way.

Did you consider this approach? I'm curious what you think about the tradeoffs involved?
